facts.fact: The countryside south of the pass is ordinary working farmland — wells, cabbage rows, grain fields, washing lines, and kitchen smoke as expected signs of life.

By the time the party approaches Millhaven, the contamination is already disrupting that rural economy at the household level: crops are rotting or torn up, wells stand untended, and hearths have gone cold.
Because the wrongness is in runoff, root, game, and flesh, the food chain itself is now compromised rather than merely the magical environment.
Chapter 3 ties the outbreak to local grain commerce as a weak but persistent clue: villagers argued over price, spoiled sacks, and a newer trader selling cheaply enough to bypass caution.
Nearby settlements can still be commercially active even while Millhaven is lost; Paret's empty wagon from Harbrook proves ordinary trade is continuing beside the disaster.
Chapter 4 confirms that bread and common flour are not background domestic goods but the working infrastructure of spread inside a living village.
Households that keep separate oats or rely less on bakery flour show lighter sickness, meaning diet and buying pattern materially affect exposure.
Granaries, store marks, and shipment ledgers are now proven to be load-bearing evidence of the plague route rather than neutral trade details.
Price undercutting is part of the threat pattern: cheaper grain is a delivery advantage for the enemy, not just ordinary market competition.

Chapter 5 proves that inns, weigh houses, depot clerks, loading tallies, locked-storage requests, and wagon marks are part of the plague infrastructure because they preserve the paper trail of grain movement.
The network targets dependence as much as geography: settlements that rely on bought flour and older stock read as the deeper exposure points.
Andorhal's importance is now economic as well as narrative; it functions as the pinch-point where multiple feeder roads and storehouse lines compress the kingdom's grain traffic.
Chapter 6 shows Andorhal functioning openly as a grain metropolis: moving gate lines, stacked sacks, storehouses, catwalk labor, and wagon traffic still feeding the kingdom.
No visible grain inspections are happening on the Andorhal approach, which means ordinary commercial throughput is still treating the plague network as normal supply.

Chapter 7 shows Andorhal's commercial routine as part of the threat architecture: paperwork lanes, counting houses, repack rooms, loading yards, and spoilage cover all help weaponized grain move as ordinary trade.

Chapter 8 proves Andorhal's commercial life is not merely cover for the plague but the working delivery mechanism itself: loading yards, bakeries, storehouses, and garrison contracts are already downstream of the treated grain.
The grain has already moved beyond Andorhal into northern Lordaeron, which means the economic network distributes the damage faster than any city-level containment can reverse it.
A still-busy grain city is more dangerous than a ruined one because ordinary throughput keeps hiding the weapon inside legitimate commerce.
The plague machine is using ordinary estate roads, private stores, and lightly cycled rural traffic as part of its Eastweald infrastructure.

Productive farm country remains economically legible even while it is being converted into hostile logistics ground, which is why the fresh sites look like workable rural property instead of theatrical ruin.
Chapter 10 confirms Barov prosperity is part of the concealment strategy: active labor, functioning yards, and old-money upkeep help plague infrastructure hide inside legitimate estate economics.

The ordinary food economy remains live deep into the crisis: wagons still move, ovens still matter, and civilians still head home for supper on roads the protagonists now understand as plague vectors.
Granaries, bakehouses, and holding yards are no longer neutral civic infrastructure in chapter 14; they are the decisive economic choke points where a city may still be saved or lost.

Stratholme's danger is inseparable from normal throughput: the city is still functioning as a consumer and distributor of grain at the very moment the protagonists race to stop it from taking the next dose.
Refugees are still fleeing Hearthglen loaded, which means ordinary household goods and food stores remain part of crisis movement rather than post-collapse emptiness.
In chapter 15 the decisive economic infrastructure is urban: granaries, bakehouses, warehouse lanes, and bread distribution inside Stratholme.

Chapter 16 proves the last-mile food economy is the real disaster point: once bread has moved from warehouses into church kitchens, homes, and ordinary hands, the city has effectively consumed the network.
Warehouse sabotage can still save portions of a city, but not the whole of it once normal household consumption has already begun.
Grain carters, service routes, kitchens, and market access are all part of the same urban distribution web that makes Stratholme impossible to save cleanly once contamination is widespread.
A fleeing grain carter can still give useful routing intelligence, showing that commercial movement remains the city’s most legible map even during collapse.
The Culling only becomes thinkable because the contaminated grain economy has already crossed from warehouses into ordinary household consumption.
